questions.question_text: An SC licensee changes brokerage firms. What must the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Notify the Commission of the change
distractors: Wait until the next renewal to update
Have the new BIC notify the Commission
Nothing if still in the same county
questions.explanation: Licensees must report changes in name, address, or brokerage firm to the Commission. This is the licensee's responsibility, not the BIC's, and must be done promptly — not at renewal.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a recognized license status in South Carolina?
distractors: Probationary
Conditional
Temporary
questions.explanation: SC recognizes the following license statuses: Active, Inactive, Current, Lapsed, Expired, Cancelled, Revoked, and Suspended. Lapsed occurs when a license is not renewed on time.

questions.question_text: After a license is revoked by the SCREC, what must the former licensee do to practice again?
distractors: Wait one year and the license auto-reinstates
Complete 30 hours of remedial education
Pay a reinstatement fine to the SCREC
questions.explanation: After revocation, the former licensee must go through the entire application process again to obtain a new license. Revocation is the most severe disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: Who bears the responsibility for ensuring an SC license is renewed on time?
distractors: The Broker-in-Charge
The Department of LLR
The brokerage firm's office manager
questions.explanation: The licensee is personally responsible for timely renewal. Neither the BIC nor the Commission is obligated to remind the licensee. Failure to renew results in lapsed or expired status.

questions.question_text: An associate recently moved to a new address. How should this be handled under SC law?
questions.correct_answer: The associate must notify the Commission
distractors: No action needed until license renewal
The BIC must update the associate's record
The associate updates through the MLS only
questions.explanation: Licensees must notify the Commission of changes in name, address, or brokerage firm. This is the licensee's duty and must be done promptly, not deferred to the next renewal cycle.

questions.question_text: Under the 2024 Practice Act, there are only two legal ways to market a property in SC. What are they?
questions.correct_answer: Ownership or active written listing agreement
distractors: Ownership or verbal authorization from seller
Listing agreement or buyer agency agreement
MLS listing or direct mail advertising only
questions.explanation: The 2024 Practice Act limits property marketing to two methods: having your name on the deed (ownership) or having an active written listing agreement with the deedholder. Assisting someone in selling property they don't own is grounds for discipline.

questions.question_text: A friend asks an unlicensed person to help negotiate the sale of their home for a fee. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, negotiating sales for compensation requires a license
distractors: Yes, if the fee is under $500
Yes, if it is a one-time transaction
Yes, if the person has power of attorney
questions.explanation: Negotiating real estate sales for compensation requires licensure in SC. Unlicensed persons may not receive compensation for performing licensed activities. Power of attorney is a separate exemption for the principal's own property.

questions.question_text: Which activity requires an SC real estate license?
distractors: Selling your own personal residence
Managing your own rental property
Serving as a court-appointed trustee
questions.explanation: Negotiating leases for compensation requires licensure. Selling your own property, managing your own rentals, and acting as a court-appointed fiduciary are all exempt from the licensing requirement.

questions.question_text: Under the 2024 Practice Act, the term 'salesperson' has been officially changed to which title?
distractors: Agent
Licensee
Sales associate
questions.explanation: The 2024 Practice Act renamed 'salesperson' to 'associate' (or 'real estate associate'). This was part of a broader update that also addressed wholesaling and team naming rules.

questions.question_text: A licensee helps a friend sell a house the friend does not own, without a listing agreement. What is the likely consequence?
distractors: A warning letter with no further action
A fine payable to the property owner
No consequence if no commission was paid
questions.explanation: Under the 2024 Practice Act, assisting someone in marketing property they do not own without a listing agreement is grounds for discipline. Only ownership or an active written listing agreement authorizes marketing.

questions.question_text: Which person is exempt from SC real estate licensing requirements?
questions.correct_answer: An attorney acting within legal practice
distractors: A real estate investor buying for resale
A part-time referral agent earning fees
A property manager collecting rent for others
questions.explanation: Attorneys acting within the scope of their legal practice are exempt from real estate licensing. Other exemptions include owners selling their own property, court-appointed fiduciaries, and government employees in official capacity.

questions.question_text: An executor is selling a deceased person's home as part of estate administration. Does the executor need an SC real estate license?
distractors: Yes, all real estate sales require a license
Only if the property exceeds $100,000
Only if the executor receives compensation
questions.explanation: Court-appointed fiduciaries such as executors, administrators, and trustees are exempt from SC real estate licensing requirements when acting within the scope of their fiduciary duty.

questions.question_text: A government employee lists and sells surplus state-owned land as part of official duties. Is a license required?
questions.correct_answer: No, government employees acting officially are exempt
distractors: Yes, all real estate sales require a license
Only if the property exceeds $500,000
Only if the sale involves a private buyer
questions.explanation: Government employees acting in their official capacity are exempt from SC real estate licensing requirements. This applies to selling government-owned property as part of their job duties.

questions.question_text: An owner wants to sell their own home without using a real estate licensee. Under SC law, this is:
distractors: Prohibited in all circumstances
Allowed only for homes under $250,000
Allowed only if a disclosure form is filed
questions.explanation: Owners selling their own property are exempt from the licensing requirement. They may sell without a license, though they must still comply with property condition disclosure requirements.

questions.question_text: A person holds power of attorney for an elderly relative and sells the relative's home. Is licensure required?
questions.correct_answer: No, persons acting under power of attorney are exempt
distractors: Yes, the person must have a PM license
Yes, unless the relative is present at closing
Only if the person charges a fee for services
questions.explanation: Persons acting under a valid power of attorney are exempt from SC real estate licensing. The exemption applies because they are acting on behalf of the principal, not for separate compensation.
